stem: A feature flag has been enabled for months and now controls core behavior in production. What should the team do next?
options: Treat the flag as technical debt: assign an owner, remove the unused branch, and delete the flag after verifying all environments use the intended behavior.
Keep the flag forever because old flags make future rollbacks easier.
Rename the flag so fewer people notice it in configuration files.
Move the flag value into a spreadsheet and remove it from deployment tooling.
explanation: Long-lived flags create hidden complexity and untested paths. Once behavior is settled, teams should clean up the flag and dead branch through an owned removal process.

stem: A shared Kubernetes namespace uses ResourceQuota, but one workload with high priority evicts lower-priority pods from other teams. What should platform engineering review?
options: Tenant isolation boundaries, PriorityClass usage, quotas, and whether critical priority is restricted to approved workloads.
Only node CPU size, because priority cannot affect eviction behavior.
The pod image registry, because private images prevent priority-based eviction.
The namespace description, because descriptions determine eviction order.
explanation: Priority classes affect scheduling and eviction. Shared namespaces need controlled priority use plus quotas and isolation. Hardware alone does not fix policy misuse.

stem: Postmortem action items are created, but the same incident recurs three months later. What process gap is most likely?
options: Action items were not tracked to verified remediation with owners, due criteria, and regression checks.
The postmortem was blameless, so nobody was punished enough to remember it.
The incident title was too short to be searchable.
The team used too many graphs during the review.
explanation: Learning requires closing corrective actions and verifying prevention. Blame does not improve systems, and title length or graph count are not the core recurrence control.

stem: A service restart loop is suspected to come from dependency startup order. Logs show only process exit codes. What should be added first?
options: Structured startup logs and readiness checks that identify which dependency check failed before the process exited.
More pod replicas, because more restarts make the pattern clearer.
A shorter restart backoff so failures reproduce faster.
A dashboard showing only total container restarts by namespace.
explanation: Startup failures need dependency-level evidence and readiness semantics. More replicas or faster restarts can amplify the problem, and aggregate restart counts lack root-cause detail.

stem: An engineer used break-glass access during an incident, but the elevated role stayed active for days. What control was missing?
options: Automatic expiry and access review for emergency roles, with session logging and post-incident reconciliation.
A longer password for the emergency role.
A team chat message announcing that access was used.
A rule that emergency access can never be used during business hours.
explanation: Break-glass access should be temporary, logged, and reviewed. Long-lived elevated access after an incident defeats least privilege.

stem: A Helm chart renders different manifests depending on local developer values files, causing production drift. What should be standardized?
options: Environment-specific values stored and reviewed in source control, with CI rendering diffs for the exact production release inputs.
Let developers keep local values files and trust them to match production.
Render manifests only after deployment, because pre-rendered diffs can be noisy.
Use random values at install time so drift is evenly distributed.
explanation: Helm inputs are part of the release. Values should be versioned, reviewed, and rendered in CI for visibility. Local-only values create unreviewed drift.

stem: A base image update fixes critical CVEs but changes the operating system package manager. What rollout is safest?
options: Rebuild images, run regression and startup tests, verify runtime dependencies, and canary the updated image before broad rollout.
Patch running containers manually and skip image rebuilds to reduce risk.
Ignore the update because package-manager changes always mean application behavior is broken.
Switch every service to the new base image in one production deployment.
explanation: Base image updates need rebuilt artifacts and validation, especially when OS dependencies change. Manual live patching and one-shot global rollout are unsafe.

stem: A low-risk documentation deploy waits for the same approval queue as a database migration. What change improves flow without weakening safety?
options: Classify changes by risk and automate low-risk approvals while keeping stricter gates for high-impact production changes.
Remove all approvals for every change type.
Require database migration approval for documentation because consistency is simpler.
Let engineers bypass approval whenever they say the change is urgent.
explanation: Risk-based change control preserves safety where it matters while avoiding needless bottlenecks for low-risk work. Blanket removal or blanket strictness is a poor tradeoff.

stem: A Terraform module introduces a required input without a default, breaking many consuming repositories. What would have prevented this?
options: A versioned module release with consumer compatibility tests and a major-version boundary for breaking input changes.
Publishing the change directly to the main branch because consumers should adapt quickly.
Removing the changelog so teams do not worry about migration details.
Using shorter variable names so missing inputs are easier to type.
explanation: Shared modules need versioned contracts and compatibility testing. Required input changes are breaking and should be managed through explicit versioning and migration notes.

stem: Tail latency spikes occur only when a few instances enter CPU throttling, while averages remain normal. What should the rollout system monitor?
options: Per-instance or per-pod saturation and high-percentile latency, not only fleet-wide averages.
Only the deployment duration, because slow deployments cause all latency spikes.
The number of Git commits in the release.
Average memory across the fleet, because CPU throttling is always reflected in memory.
explanation: Fleet averages hide localized saturation. Per-instance signals and high-percentile latency expose tail impact during rollout.

stem: A release manifest records the Git SHA but not the container digest. Later, the tag was moved and nobody can prove what actually ran. What should be fixed?
options: Record immutable artifact digests alongside source SHA and configuration version for every deployment.
Use only mutable tags because they are human-readable.
Store the release manager's name instead of artifact identity.
Rely on the repository default branch to reconstruct deployed images.
explanation: Git SHA does not prove the built artifact if tags move or rebuilds occur. Immutable digests give exact runtime provenance.

stem: A deployment audit log records successful completion, but not the failed health checks that happened before rollback. What should be captured?
options: Both successful and failed deployment events, including health-check evidence, rollback triggers, and final state.
Only final success events because failed attempts create clutter.
Only the human approver's name because automation details are too noisy.
No rollback events because they are not production changes.
explanation: Audit trails must include failed attempts and rollback evidence to support incident analysis and compliance. Final success alone hides important operational history.

stem: HPA scales pods based on CPU, but the bottleneck is outbound connection pool exhaustion. What metric should drive scaling or admission control?
options: A workload-relevant signal such as queue depth, request latency, or connection pool saturation, with safeguards against scaling beyond downstream capacity.
CPU only, because every bottleneck eventually becomes CPU-bound.
The number of Kubernetes namespaces in the cluster.
Container image size, because larger images need more replicas.
explanation: Autoscaling should follow the actual bottleneck or demand signal. CPU may not reflect connection saturation, and scaling can worsen downstream overload without capacity guards.

stem: Production deploy credentials are protected, but a staging job can overwrite production artifacts in the shared registry. What should be changed?
options: Separate artifact namespaces or permissions so lower environments cannot mutate production-release artifacts.
Trust staging jobs because they do not have production cluster credentials.
Use the same mutable tag for all environments to simplify cleanup.
Let staging overwrite artifacts but require production to pull faster.
explanation: Artifact integrity is part of production security. Staging jobs should not overwrite production artifacts even if they cannot deploy directly.

stem: A new service extraction succeeds technically, but teams cannot tell which service owns a failed customer workflow. What should be established?
options: Clear service ownership, request tracing across boundaries, and runbooks that map customer workflows to responsible services.
One shared on-call rotation for every service forever.
No ownership boundaries until the migration is fully complete.
Only infrastructure dashboards, because service ownership is a people issue.
explanation: Microservice extraction needs operational ownership and observability across workflows. Without that, incidents become coordination failures.

stem: A pinned CI action SHA is secure today, but the team never reviews updates. What risk remains?
options: The pipeline can become stale and miss security fixes, so dependency update automation and review cadence are still needed.
No risk remains because pinned SHAs are permanently safe.
Pinned actions automatically update when vendors publish patches.
The only risk is that action names become harder to read.
explanation: Pinning prevents surprise changes but also freezes dependencies. Teams need a controlled update process to receive fixes.

stem: An online migration batches updates, but each batch still creates replication lag that affects read replicas. What should be tuned?
options: Batch size, pause interval, transaction scope, and replica-lag monitoring with automatic throttling.
Only the migration script filename, because replicas prioritize shorter names.
Disable replica monitoring so alerts do not interrupt the migration.
Run all remaining batches in one transaction to finish sooner.
explanation: Online migrations must respect downstream replication capacity. Batch size and throttling should adapt to replica lag and lock behavior.

stem: A runbook tells responders to restart a service, but not how to verify whether the restart solved user impact. What is missing?
options: Post-mitigation validation steps tied to user-facing health metrics or synthetic journeys.
A longer explanation of who wrote the service originally.
A command to restart the service twice for confidence.
A note saying responders should watch logs until they feel comfortable.
explanation: Runbooks need clear validation criteria after mitigation. User-facing metrics or synthetics prove recovery better than vague log watching.

stem: Staging and production credentials are separated, but both environments share the same third-party webhook endpoint. Staging tests triggered real customer notifications. What should be changed?
options: Use environment-isolated third-party endpoints or test modes, and enforce configuration validation before deployment.
Keep the shared endpoint and tell testers not to click notification paths.
Mask webhook URLs in logs only.
Disable all staging tests that touch notifications.
explanation: Environment isolation must include external integrations, not only cloud credentials. Test modes and validation prevent staging from causing production side effects.

stem: A PodDisruptionBudget allows one eviction, but the service has only one replica during off-hours due to autoscaling. Node maintenance still causes downtime. What should be adjusted?
options: Coordinate minimum replicas and disruption budgets so voluntary maintenance cannot evict the last available pod.
Set maxUnavailable to 100 percent because off-hours traffic is lower.
Delete the PDB only during maintenance and recreate it later manually.
Rely on clients to retry until autoscaling creates a new pod.
explanation: Availability controls must align with autoscaling minima. A PDB cannot preserve availability if only one replica exists and maintenance evicts it.

stem: Canary analysis compares latency between baseline and canary, but the canary receives only low-complexity requests. What should be improved?
options: Route representative traffic or segment analysis by request class so canary and baseline comparisons are fair.
Promote immediately because lower latency proves the canary is better.
Send only health checks to canary because they are safest.
Disable baseline comparison and use canary CPU only.
explanation: Canary analysis requires comparable traffic. Biased routing can hide regressions for complex requests, so request-class segmentation or representative routing is needed.

stem: Preview environments are cleaned up by TTL, but some contain test databases with sensitive copied production data. What policy is needed?
options: Prevent production-sensitive data in previews or apply approved anonymization, access controls, and lifecycle deletion for all preview data stores.
Keep the data because previews are temporary and therefore low risk.
Extend TTL so teams have more time to inspect sensitive data.
Make preview URLs unguessable and skip data controls.
explanation: Temporary environments still need data protection. Sensitive production data should be avoided or anonymized, access-controlled, and deleted with environment lifecycle.

stem: A Kubernetes cluster has many custom controllers. During API-server degradation, controllers retry rapidly and make the outage worse. What should controller authors implement?
options: Client-side rate limits, exponential backoff with jitter, efficient watches, and workqueue retry bounds.
Tight retry loops so desired state converges faster.
Full cluster list calls every second to avoid missed events.
More verbose logging inside every retry loop as the only mitigation.
explanation: Controllers should be polite API clients. Backoff, rate limits, efficient watches, and bounded retries prevent retry storms during degradation.

stem: A non-root pod policy is enabled, but images still fail because applications expect writable root filesystems. What is the right fix?
options: Update images and manifests to write only to approved writable paths such as mounted volumes or /tmp, while keeping non-root policy.
Disable non-root policy for every workload because one image failed.
Run the container as root and rely on namespace isolation only.
Make the root filesystem writable and privileged so legacy paths work.
explanation: Security policy adoption often requires image and filesystem changes. The fix is to adapt workloads while preserving the control, not broadly disable it.

stem: Read-only reporting credentials accidentally gain write permissions after a migration. What would catch this earlier?
options: Automated permission tests or policy checks that verify database roles retain expected grants after migrations.
A dashboard of query counts only.
Naming the role readonly and assuming the database enforces the name.
Waiting for analysts to report unexpected write ability.
explanation: Role permissions are testable contracts. Automated checks after migrations catch grant drift that names and usage dashboards cannot enforce.

stem: A config change is rolled back, but some pods keep the old value because they cache it in memory. What should the config system define?
options: Reload semantics, cache TTLs, and rollout or restart behavior for config changes and rollbacks.
That all config is instant because files changed on disk.
That pods should never cache configuration for performance.
That rollback is complete when Git is reverted, regardless of running processes.
explanation: Runtime configuration needs explicit freshness behavior. Git or file changes do not guarantee running processes have reloaded values.

stem: Incident count is decreasing, but customer-impact minutes are increasing. What does this suggest?
options: Reliability reporting should include severity and impact duration, because fewer incidents can still mean worse customer experience.
The team is definitely improving because count is the only reliability measure.
Customer-impact minutes should be ignored because they are hard to calculate.
Incident titles are probably too long.
explanation: Incident count alone can mislead. Severity, duration, and customer impact reveal whether reliability is actually improving.

stem: A spot-node workload is marked interruption-tolerant, but jobs are not checkpointed and restart from zero after interruption. What should be added?
options: Checkpointing or idempotent progress tracking so interrupted work resumes without losing all completed effort.
Higher pod priority so spot nodes are never interrupted.
A rule that spot nodes are used only during weekends.
A larger container image cache to prevent interruption.
explanation: Interruptible workloads need recoverable progress. Pod priority does not prevent cloud capacity interruption, and scheduling windows or image cache do not preserve work.

stem: A team wants to decommission an old production service. What should happen before removal?
options: Verify no live traffic or dependencies remain, communicate owners, preserve needed data, and keep a rollback or restoration path for the decommission window.
Delete it immediately if CPU usage is low.
Remove monitoring first so alerts do not fire during deletion.
Rename the service and wait for users to complain.
explanation: Safe decommissioning requires dependency checks, communication, data retention, and rollback planning. Low CPU alone does not prove unused.

stem: A frozen dependency install still downloads packages from the public internet during builds. What supply-chain improvement should be considered?
options: Use a trusted package mirror or artifact cache with integrity verification and controlled egress from build runners.
Allow public downloads because lockfiles alone prove every package source is safe.
Disable checksum verification to reduce build failures.
Commit node_modules or vendor directories for every project without review.
explanation: Reproducible installs also need trusted sources and integrity. Mirrors or caches reduce external dependency risk. Lockfiles alone do not protect every supply-chain path.

stem: A service mesh policy rollout blocks traffic because one namespace lacked required labels. What should be added before future rollouts?
options: Preflight policy checks against target namespaces and progressive rollout with telemetry before broad enforcement.
Immediate cluster-wide enforcement so labels are fixed under pressure.
A manual Slack poll asking which namespaces have labels.
Disabling all labels because labels caused the outage.
explanation: Policy rollout should validate prerequisites and expand gradually. Cluster-wide enforcement without preflight increases blast radius.

stem: Ephemeral CI runners are destroyed after jobs, but build caches persist and are writable by untrusted jobs. What risk remains?
options: Cache poisoning; caches need trust boundaries, keys, and validation before trusted jobs consume them.
No risk remains because the runner VM is destroyed.
The only risk is higher cache storage cost.
Trusted jobs automatically ignore cache contents from untrusted branches.
explanation: Persistent caches can carry malicious content from untrusted jobs into trusted builds. Runner ephemerality does not protect shared caches by itself.

stem: A team defines an availability SLO using load balancer 5xx responses only. Users still see failures from client-side JavaScript errors. What should be considered?
options: Add user-facing indicators such as real-user monitoring or synthetic journeys that capture frontend failure modes.
Keep the SLO unchanged because backend 5xx is the only valid availability signal.
Count JavaScript bundle size as availability.
Ignore browser errors because they are outside server operations.
explanation: Availability should reflect user experience. Backend 5xx metrics can miss frontend failures, so RUM or synthetics may be needed.

stem: Image signature verification is enforced, but an attacker can push a signed image from an untrusted pipeline. What should policy verify too?
options: Trusted provenance or issuer identity, not just that some signature exists.
Only the image tag length, because signed tags are harder to spoof.
Whether the image was pushed during business hours.
The number of layers in the image.
explanation: A signature is useful only if it comes from a trusted identity or provenance path. Admission should verify who signed and under what build process.

stem: Certificate expiry monitoring checks only stored certificate files, but users still hit an expired certificate at the load balancer. What should be monitored?
options: The certificate actually served by public endpoints, in addition to inventory of stored certificates.
Only filesystem certificates because load balancers always reload immediately.
The certificate common name length.
Application log volume, because expired certificates always increase logs.
explanation: What matters is the certificate clients receive. Endpoint probing catches reload and binding failures that inventory alone can miss.

stem: A policy rejects hostPath mounts, but a storage driver legitimately requires one in a system namespace. What is the best policy design?
options: Use narrow, reviewed exceptions for specific service accounts or namespaces while keeping the default deny for normal workloads.
Disable the policy globally because one platform component needs an exception.
Allow hostPath whenever the pod name contains storage.
Tell application teams to request cluster-admin when they need hostPath.
explanation: Security policy needs safe exception mechanisms for platform components. Global disablement or name-based exceptions are too broad.

stem: A pipeline is faster after moving tests to post-merge, but broken code reaches the shared branch frequently. What balance should be restored?
options: Keep fast, high-signal pre-merge gates and move only expensive or lower-risk checks post-merge with rapid revert paths.
Move every test post-merge because speed is always the priority.
Require no pre-merge validation if developers promise to run tests locally.
Block merges only when production customers report defects.
explanation: Pre-merge gates should protect shared branches with high-signal checks. Post-merge checks are useful but cannot replace all guardrails.

stem: A readiness probe checks only process liveness, so pods receive traffic before database migrations finish. What should readiness represent?
options: Whether the pod is ready to serve real traffic, including critical dependencies or startup prerequisites needed for requests.
Only whether the process has a PID.
Whether the pod was created less than one minute ago.
Whether the container image exists in the registry.
explanation: Readiness should indicate ability to handle traffic. Process existence is liveness, not readiness for dependency-backed requests.

stem: An SBOM is generated at build time, but not stored with the release artifact. During an advisory, teams cannot find the relevant SBOM. What should change?
options: Attach or index the SBOM by immutable artifact digest and retain it with release provenance.
Generate SBOMs only on developer laptops because they are easier to inspect there.
Store one generic SBOM for the whole company.
Delete SBOMs after deployment because vulnerabilities are future events.
explanation: SBOMs must be discoverable by artifact identity. Build-time generation without retention breaks vulnerability response.

stem: An operator watches all secrets cluster-wide but needs only one label-selected subset. What is the security and scalability concern?
options: Overbroad watches increase API load and expose sensitive data beyond the operator's real need; scope RBAC and selectors narrowly.
Watching more objects is always safer because the operator has more context.
Secrets are never returned through Kubernetes watches.
The only concern is that log lines become longer.
explanation: Controllers should watch only needed resources. Overbroad secret access creates both load and confidentiality risk.

stem: A rollback plan says redeploy the previous version, but does not say when to abandon rollback and roll forward. What should be added?
options: Decision criteria for rollback versus roll-forward, based on data compatibility, customer impact, and recovery time.
A rule to always rollback no matter what changed.
A rule to always roll forward because rollback is embarrassing.
Only the name of the deployment command.
explanation: Some failures are safer to roll forward, especially after data changes. Recovery plans need criteria, not one universal command.

stem: Metrics cost exploded after adding labels for full exception messages. What should be done?
options: Move exception detail to logs or traces and use bounded error class or code labels in metrics.
Keep full messages as labels because they make graphs self-explanatory.
Hash the full message into a unique label for every error instance.
Disable metrics and rely only on screenshots of dashboards.
explanation: Metrics require bounded cardinality. Detailed exception text belongs in logs or traces, while metrics should use stable error categories.

stem: A hotfix branch was merged back, but production still differs because operators edited a runtime config file directly. What should be reconciled?
options: Runtime configuration source of truth, with direct edits captured in versioned config or reverted through the deployment system.
Only application source code, because runtime configuration is not part of production state.
The operator's shell history, because it is enough source of truth.
Nothing, because production is working now.
explanation: Production state includes runtime configuration. Direct edits must be captured in the managed source or reverted to avoid drift.

stem: A stateful canary writes new-format records that old pods cannot read. What release strategy is safer?
options: Deploy compatibility changes first so old and new versions can read both formats before any canary writes new-format records.
Canary the writer first because fewer pods means lower risk.
Force old pods to crash so traffic moves to new pods faster.
Disable reads during the canary so incompatibility is hidden.
explanation: Stateful rollout must preserve read/write compatibility while versions coexist. Writer-first canaries can poison data for old readers.

stem: Disk usage grows quickly from debug logs after a verbose flag is enabled. What guardrail would limit future impact?
options: Log volume budgets, retention controls, and alerts on growth rate before disks approach full.
Only a larger disk, because more space is the main reliability control.
A policy that debug logs can never be used in production under any circumstance.
Waiting for log rotation to handle every burst automatically.
explanation: Verbose logs need volume and retention guardrails plus early alerts. Larger disks may help but do not prevent runaway growth.

stem: A shared CI template update changes default deployment behavior. Some repositories opt in accidentally. What would reduce surprise?
options: Explicit version pinning and migration notes, with repositories choosing when to adopt the new major template behavior.
Automatically forcing all repositories to the newest template revision.
Hiding template changes from consuming teams until after rollout.
Removing version history so only one template exists.
explanation: Shared templates are dependencies. Version pinning and deliberate adoption reduce unexpected behavior changes across repositories.

stem: Clients keep using failed zone endpoints because they cache DNS longer than intended. What should be addressed?
options: Client DNS caching behavior, connection reuse, endpoint health handling, and retry logic across healthy targets.
Only authoritative DNS TTL, because all clients obey it exactly.
The number of deployment jobs in CI.
The application logo cache headers.
explanation: Real clients and runtimes may cache DNS or connections differently. Resilience requires client behavior that stops using failed targets.

stem: Production Terraform applies run only from CI, but the CI plan output is not reviewed before apply. What safety gate is missing?
options: Plan review or policy evaluation for production changes before apply, especially for destructive or high-risk actions.
Local laptop applies, because manual execution is safer than CI.
Skipping plan entirely so applies are faster.
Only checking whether the pipeline file changed.
explanation: Centralized apply is good, but production plans still need review or policy checks to catch destructive changes before execution.

stem: A self-healing restart masks a memory leak for weeks until traffic grows and recovery loops fail. What should observability include?
options: Signals for restart frequency, memory trend, and repeated self-healing actions so automation does not hide chronic defects.
Only current uptime, because restarts that recover are not incidents.
A rule to suppress every alert after an automated restart.
No restart metrics because they make dashboards noisy.
explanation: Self-healing should be observable. Repeated automated recovery can indicate latent defects and capacity risk.

stem: GitOps applies reviewed manifests, but cluster operators can still edit production resources manually with cluster-admin. What should be tightened?
options: Restrict direct cluster-admin access, define break-glass workflows, and rely on GitOps controllers for normal production mutations.
Keep cluster-admin for everyone because GitOps will eventually revert mistakes.
Disable GitOps because manual edits are faster.
Allow manual edits only if operators promise to commit them later.
explanation: GitOps governance is weakened by broad direct mutation access. Normal changes should flow through Git, with audited break-glass for emergencies.
